aliases: Steamed Clams with Lemongrass and Herbs

english: Steamed Clams with Lemongrass and Herbs

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general clam food product class; no specific entry for Vietnamese steamed clams with lemongrass. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked clams and cockles; no specific Vietnamese preparation code exists. Steamed preparation with lemongrass is a preparation variant not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steamed clams with lemongrass as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of steamed clams with lemongrass is approximately 400 g, typically containing a generous portion of clams in aromatic broth with lemongrass stalks and fresh herbs.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Clams, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175159) combined with lemongrass and condiment adjustment for Vietnamese preparation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese steamed clams with lemongrass exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked clams (moist heat, FDC ID 175159) was used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values were adapted to reflect the dish composition: clams (~70% by weight), lemongrass broth, fish sauce, garlic, chilli, and fresh herbs. Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked clams due to fish sauce contribution. Input record values (energy 62.5 kcal, protein 6.25 g, fat 2.0 g, carbohydrate 3.75 g) are broadly consistent with USDA cooked clam data adjusted for dish dilution and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300 mg/100g is plausible given fish sauce use. All values documented as adapted estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; shellfish and low-carbohydrate seafood analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese steamed clams with lemongrass. Clams are a very low-carbohydrate shellfish (3.75 g carbohydrate per 100 g in this dish). GI is estimated at approximately 5 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of shellfish and low-carbohydrate seafood preparations as having negligible glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (5 × 15 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.75, classified as very low. This estimate is consistent with Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283 analogous seafood category.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 300 mg per 100 g, primarily from fish sauce, contributes approximately 1200 mg sodium per standard 400 g bowl serving — 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or a low-sodium substitute, and avoid adding extra salt; clam-derived potassium partially offsets sodium load but does not eliminate the concern at this serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.75 g per 100 g) and high protein (6.25 g per 100 g) result in minimal glycaemic impact with an estimated GI of approximately 5 and glycaemic load of 0.75 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed clams with lemongrass is a suitable protein-rich, low-glycaemic choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; fish sauce contributes trace sugars with negligible glycaemic impact, and steaming preserves nutrients without adding refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is very low at 0.5 g per 100 g; cholesterol is moderate at 17.5 mg per 100 g (70 mg per serving); the dish provides lean protein and anti-inflammatory lemongrass compounds.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat profile and lean shellfish protein make this dish broadly suitable for cardiovascular health; however, the elevated sodium from fish sauce warrants caution for individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease, and portion control is advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lemongrass contains citral and limonene with documented anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is very low at 0.5 g per 100 g; steaming avoids advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory herb profile and low saturated fat; patients sensitive to nightshades (chilli) should monitor individual response and request chilli omission if needed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Clams with Lemongrass (Vietnamese: Nghêu Hấp Sả) is a classic Vietnamese seafood dish in which fresh clams are steamed over or with aromatic lemongrass stalks, producing a fragrant, savoury broth. The dish typically includes garlic, chilli, fish sauce, and fresh herbs such as Thai basil and coriander, which infuse the clams and cooking liquor with bright, citrusy, and mildly spicy flavours. The clam meat is tender and briny, with a clean oceanic taste complemented by the herbal warmth of lemongrass. Steaming is the defining preparation method, preserving the natural sweetness of the clams while avoiding added fats. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (6.25 g per 100 g), low in fat (2.0 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (3.75 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycaemic, nutrient-dense seafood option. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce, which is clinically relevant for hypertension management. Calcium (25 mg per 100 g) and iron (1.25 mg per 100 g) are contributed by the clam meat. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is widely consumed in Vietnam and Vietnamese restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, and is commonly served as a shared starter or main dish. The lemongrass and herb components provide polyphenol antioxidants and anti-inflammatory aromatic compounds, adding functional food value beyond basic macronutrients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese steamed clams with lemongrass was found in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked clam data (FDC ID 175159) and are broadly consistent with the input record values. Medium confidence reflects the adapted source match and the inherent variability in fish sauce sodium contribution across restaurant preparations.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this Vietnamese steamed clam dish preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any approved regional database as of 2026-04-16; the USDA cooked clam entry does not report these fractions at the dish level after dilution with broth and herbs. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the composite dish is not reported in the adapted USDA source at this preparation level; clams are a known potassium source but a reliable per-100g figure for the dish as prepared cannot be confirmed. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese dish preparation are not available in approved sources at the dish level as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible for a steamed shellfish preparation with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA cooked clam data. Input record data_source was listed as 'grabfood' with provenance 'ai_generated'; all nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved source. The input HPB source URL (https://foodo.sg/) did not provide a direct nutrient match for this dish and is not used as the primary nutrient evidence source.
